title: Too Much Screen Time?
summary: Colleagues David and Emma debate whether excessive screen time is harmful. David worries about attention spans and mental health, while Emma argues the real issue is how — not how much — we use our screens.

body.speaker: David
body.text: Did you see that report this morning? The one saying that teenagers are spending an average of eight hours a day on screens. Eight hours, Emma.

body.speaker: Emma
body.text: I did see it. But I think we need to be careful about how we interpret that figure. Not all screen time is the same. Reading an article online is very different from scrolling through social media for three hours.

body.speaker: David
body.text: That's true, but the sheer volume is still concerning. I've noticed my own ability to focus has got worse over the last few years. I struggle to read a book for more than twenty minutes before I feel the urge to check my phone.

body.speaker: Emma
body.text: I know what you mean — I've experienced that too. But I'm not sure screens are entirely to blame. Life has just become more fragmented generally. Open-plan offices, constant notifications, meetings that could have been emails. The problem might be broader than screens.

body.speaker: David
body.text: Maybe. But there's pretty compelling evidence that social media in particular is linked to rising rates of anxiety and depression, especially in young people. Platforms are literally designed to keep you hooked. That's not neutral technology — that's a deliberate design choice.

body.speaker: Emma
body.text: I don't disagree that some platforms are exploitative. But banning screens or reducing time arbitrarily doesn't solve the underlying issue, which is that people — especially kids — aren't being taught how to use them well. Digital literacy should be as fundamental as reading and writing.

body.speaker: David
body.text: Digital literacy, yes — but also just limiting usage. A lot of the research suggests that the happiest teenagers are those who spend significant time offline: in sport, in face-to-face conversation, in nature. Real-world engagement seems to be protective.

body.speaker: Emma
body.text: I completely agree with that. But the same research often shows that moderate screen use — say, one to two hours of social media — has no measurable negative effect on wellbeing. The dose makes the poison, as they say.

body.speaker: David
body.text: The dose makes the poison — sure. But who decides what a healthy dose looks like when the apps are optimised to make you consume as much as possible? It's naive to say 'just use it in moderation' when the whole product is designed to undermine moderation.

body.speaker: Emma
body.text: That's fair. I think the responsibility does lie partly with the platforms. But I also think adults and parents can model better behaviour. If children see their parents constantly on their phones at the dinner table, what message does that send?

body.speaker: David
body.text: A terrible one. I'll admit I'm guilty of that myself. I think the honest answer is that we're all still figuring this out — society, parents, educators, tech companies. It's a very new problem.

body.speaker: Emma
body.text: Exactly. And I think the worst thing we can do is panic and overreact. Nuanced thinking — like what you're doing — is far more useful than blanket bans or moral panics.

body.speaker: David
body.text: Well, thank you. Though I suspect you might be complimenting me just to win the argument.

body.speaker: Emma
body.text: Maybe a little. But I do mean it.

annotations.meaningVi: khối lượng thuần túy, số lượng đơn thuần quá lớn
annotations.exampleEn: The sheer volume of information online is overwhelming.

annotations.meaningVi: sự thôi thúc, cảm giác muốn
annotations.exampleEn: She felt the urge to check her messages every few minutes.

annotations.meaningVi: bị phân mảnh, không liên tục
annotations.exampleEn: Modern work schedules have become increasingly fragmented.
There is compelling evidence that exercise improves mental health.

annotations.meaningVi: giữ bạn nghiện, khiến bạn không thể dừng lại
annotations.exampleEn: The game was designed to keep you hooked for hours.

annotations.meaningVi: bóc lột, tận dụng người dùng một cách không công bằng
annotations.exampleEn: Some business models are seen as exploitative of vulnerable users.

annotations.meaningVi: hiểu biết kỹ thuật số, khả năng sử dụng công nghệ có hiểu biết
annotations.exampleEn: Digital literacy is now considered an essential skill for young people.

annotations.meaningVi: có tác dụng bảo vệ, phòng ngừa
annotations.exampleEn: Regular exercise is thought to be protective against depression.

annotations.meaningVi: liều lượng tạo ra chất độc (cái gì cũng có hại nếu quá nhiều)
annotations.exampleEn: Even water can be dangerous in excess — the dose makes the poison.
Coffee is fine in moderation, but too much can cause anxiety.

annotations.meaningVi: làm suy yếu, phá vỡ ngầm
annotations.exampleEn: Poor sleep can undermine your concentration at work.

annotations.meaningVi: làm gương, thể hiện hành vi tốt hơn
annotations.exampleEn: Parents can model better behaviour by putting their phones away at dinner.

annotations.meaningVi: Tôi thừa nhận tôi cũng có lỗi
annotations.exampleEn: I'll admit I'm guilty of checking emails too late at night.

annotations.meaningVi: lệnh cấm một cách cứng nhắc, toàn diện
annotations.exampleEn: Blanket bans rarely work — they just push the behaviour underground.

annotations.meaningVi: cơn hoảng loạn đạo đức, phản ứng thái quá của xã hội với một vấn đề
annotations.exampleEn: Every new technology has caused a moral panic at some point.

annotations.meaningVi: suy nghĩ có sắc thái, suy nghĩ không cực đoan
annotations.exampleEn: Nuanced thinking is more valuable than black-and-white positions.
Try not to overreact — the situation is difficult but manageable.

grammarNotes.title: Present Perfect — Recent changes and personal experience
grammarNotes.bodyVi: David dùng thì hiện tại hoàn thành để nói về những thay đổi gần đây có liên quan đến hiện tại: 'my own ability to focus has got worse over the last few years.' Khác với quá khứ đơn ('got worse last year'), hiện tại hoàn thành không xác định thời điểm cụ thể và nhấn mạnh trạng thái hiện tại. Emma cũng dùng: 'I've experienced that too.'
grammarNotes.bodyEn: David uses the present perfect to describe recent changes still relevant now: 'my own ability to focus has got worse over the last few years.' Unlike the past simple ('got worse last year'), the present perfect does not specify a time point and emphasises the current state. Emma mirrors this: 'I've experienced that too.'

grammarNotes.title: Passive Voice — Describing designed systems without personal blame
grammarNotes.bodyVi: Bài hội thoại dùng bị động để mô tả cách các nền tảng được thiết kế: 'Platforms are literally designed to keep you hooked', 'the apps are optimised to make you consume as much as possible', 'the whole product is designed to undermine moderation'. Bị động ở đây không phải để che giấu tác nhân, mà để nhấn mạnh bản chất của hệ thống hơn là trách nhiệm của cá nhân.
grammarNotes.bodyEn: The dialogue uses passive voice to describe how platforms are built: 'Platforms are literally designed to keep you hooked', 'the apps are optimised to make you consume as much as possible'. Here, passive voice is not used to hide agency but to emphasise the systemic nature of the design rather than personal responsibility.

translationVi:
David: Bạn có thấy bản báo cáo đó sáng nay không? Báo cáo nói rằng thanh thiếu niên đang dành trung bình tám tiếng mỗi ngày trên màn hình. Tám tiếng, Emma.
Emma: Tôi có thấy rồi. Nhưng tôi nghĩ chúng ta cần cẩn thận trong cách diễn giải con số đó. Không phải tất cả thời gian sử dụng màn hình đều như nhau. Đọc bài viết trực tuyến rất khác với việc lướt mạng xã hội ba tiếng đồng hồ.
David: Điều đó đúng, nhưng khối lượng thuần túy vẫn đáng lo ngại. Tôi đã nhận thấy khả năng tập trung của chính mình kém đi trong vài năm gần đây. Tôi cố đọc sách nhưng không quá hai mươi phút đã cảm thấy muốn kiểm tra điện thoại.
Emma: Tôi hiểu bạn nói gì — tôi cũng đã trải qua điều đó. Nhưng tôi không chắc màn hình hoàn toàn là nguyên nhân. Cuộc sống nói chung đã trở nên phân mảnh hơn. Văn phòng không có vách ngăn, thông báo liên tục, những cuộc họp lẽ ra có thể chỉ là email. Vấn đề có thể rộng hơn màn hình.
David: Có thể vậy. Nhưng có bằng chứng khá thuyết phục rằng mạng xã hội đặc biệt liên quan đến tỷ lệ lo âu và trầm cảm ngày càng tăng, đặc biệt ở người trẻ. Các nền tảng được thiết kế để giữ bạn nghiện. Đó không phải công nghệ trung lập — đó là một lựa chọn thiết kế có chủ ý.
Emma: Tôi không phủ nhận rằng một số nền tảng có tính bóc lột. Nhưng cấm màn hình hoặc giảm thời gian một cách tùy tiện không giải quyết được vấn đề cốt lõi, đó là người dùng — đặc biệt trẻ em — không được dạy cách sử dụng chúng tốt. Hiểu biết kỹ thuật số nên là kỹ năng cơ bản như đọc và viết.
David: Hiểu biết kỹ thuật số, đúng — nhưng cũng cần giới hạn việc sử dụng. Nhiều nghiên cứu cho thấy những thanh thiếu niên hạnh phúc nhất là những người dành nhiều thời gian ngoại tuyến: chơi thể thao, trò chuyện trực tiếp, trong thiên nhiên. Sự gắn kết với thế giới thực dường như có tác dụng bảo vệ.
Emma: Tôi hoàn toàn đồng ý với điều đó. Nhưng cùng nghiên cứu đó thường cho thấy việc sử dụng màn hình ở mức vừa phải — khoảng một đến hai tiếng mạng xã hội — không có tác động tiêu cực có thể đo lường được đối với sức khỏe tinh thần. Như người ta nói, liều lượng tạo ra chất độc.
David: Liều lượng tạo ra chất độc — được thôi. Nhưng ai quyết định liều lượng lành mạnh trông như thế nào khi các ứng dụng được tối ưu hóa để khiến bạn tiêu thụ càng nhiều càng tốt? Thật ngây thơ khi nói 'chỉ cần dùng điều độ' khi toàn bộ sản phẩm được thiết kế để phá vỡ sự điều độ.
Emma: Điều đó công bằng. Tôi nghĩ trách nhiệm có một phần nằm ở các nền tảng. Nhưng tôi cũng nghĩ người lớn và cha mẹ có thể làm gương về hành vi tốt hơn. Nếu trẻ em thấy cha mẹ luôn cầm điện thoại trên bàn ăn, điều đó truyền đạt thông điệp gì?
David: Một thông điệp tệ hại. Tôi phải thừa nhận mình cũng có lỗi về điều đó. Tôi nghĩ câu trả lời thành thật là tất cả chúng ta đang còn tự mò mẫm — xã hội, cha mẹ, nhà giáo dục, các công ty công nghệ. Đây là vấn đề rất mới.
Emma: Đúng vậy. Và tôi nghĩ điều tệ nhất chúng ta có thể làm là hoảng loạn và phản ứng thái quá. Suy nghĩ có sắc thái — như bạn đang làm — hữu ích hơn nhiều so với những lệnh cấm cứng nhắc hay cơn hoảng loạn đạo đức.
David: Ồ, cảm ơn. Mặc dù tôi nghi bạn có thể đang khen tôi chỉ để thắng cuộc tranh luận.
Emma: Có thể một chút. Nhưng tôi nói thật đấy.

questions.prompt: According to the report mentioned, how many hours a day do teenagers spend on screens on average?
questions.explanation: David says: 'teenagers are spending an average of eight hours a day on screens.'
questions.hint: David repeats the number with emphasis.

questions.prompt: What is Emma's main point about the report's figure?
options: The figure is probably exaggerated by the researchers
Not all screen time is the same — it depends on how screens are used
The figure applies only to teenagers, not adults
The figure includes time spent at school, which should not count
questions.explanation: Emma says: 'Not all screen time is the same. Reading an article online is very different from scrolling through social media for three hours.'
questions.hint: Emma makes a distinction between types of screen use.

questions.prompt: What personal experience does David share about his own attention span?
options: He can no longer concentrate in meetings
He has given up social media entirely
He struggles to read a book for more than twenty minutes before wanting to check his phone
He has noticed he makes more mistakes at work since using screens more
questions.explanation: David says: 'I struggle to read a book for more than twenty minutes before I feel the urge to check my phone.'
questions.hint: David uses a specific personal example involving reading.

questions.prompt: What does Emma suggest might also contribute to people's reduced ability to focus?
options: Too much coffee and poor sleep
Longer working hours and shorter breaks
Fragmented modern life — open-plan offices, notifications, unnecessary meetings
questions.explanation: Emma says 'Life has just become more fragmented generally. Open-plan offices, constant notifications, meetings that could have been emails.'
questions.hint: Emma offers a broader explanation beyond just screens.

questions.prompt: What does David say about how social media platforms are designed?
options: They are designed to keep users hooked — a deliberate design choice
They are designed to be educational and informative
They are designed for communication but misused by teenagers
They are designed to be addictive by accident, not on purpose
questions.explanation: David says 'Platforms are literally designed to keep you hooked. That's not neutral technology — that's a deliberate design choice.'
questions.hint: David uses the word 'literally' to emphasise his point.

questions.prompt: What solution does Emma propose instead of reducing screen time arbitrarily?
options: Taxing social media companies to fund mental health services
Teaching people — especially children — digital literacy skills
Requiring parental approval for all social media use
Limiting teenagers to devices with no internet access
questions.explanation: Emma argues that 'Digital literacy should be as fundamental as reading and writing.'
questions.hint: Emma compares her proposed solution to a traditional school subject.

questions.prompt: What does David say about the happiest teenagers, based on research?
options: They are active on social media but in a positive way
They attend schools that restrict phone use
They spend significant time offline — in sport, face-to-face conversation, and nature
They use screens primarily for creative rather than social purposes
questions.explanation: David says 'the happiest teenagers are those who spend significant time offline: in sport, in face-to-face conversation, in nature.'
questions.hint: David gives three specific examples of offline activities.

questions.prompt: What does Emma mean by 'the dose makes the poison'?
options: Social media is toxic and should be avoided entirely
The amount of time spent on screens should be regulated by law
Teenagers need smaller doses of information than adults
Any substance or activity can be harmful in excess, but is fine in moderation
questions.explanation: Emma uses this proverb to argue that moderate screen use has no measurable negative effect — it is excess that causes harm.
questions.hint: Think about what 'poison' and 'dose' mean in a medical context.

questions.prompt: What behaviour does Emma suggest parents should model for their children?
options: Not using their phones at the dinner table
Reading physical books instead of e-books
Taking regular technology breaks during the working day
Explaining to children why certain apps are harmful
questions.explanation: Emma says: 'If children see their parents constantly on their phones at the dinner table, what message does that send?'
questions.hint: Emma gives a specific example of poor adult behaviour.

questions.prompt: What does David admit at the end of the conversation?
options: That he has decided to stop using social media entirely
That he is guilty of using his phone at the dinner table himself
That he agrees with Emma's position more than he initially suggested
That the research he cited was not very reliable
questions.explanation: David says: 'I'll admit I'm guilty of that myself' — referring to using his phone at the dinner table.
questions.hint: He says this directly after Emma's point about the dinner table.

cloze.template:
David: Did you see that report this morning? The one saying that teenagers are spending an average of eight hours a day on screens. Eight hours, Emma.
Emma: I did see it. But I think we need to be careful about how we interpret that figure. Not all screen time is the same. Reading an article online is very different from {{b1}} through social media for three hours.
David: That's true, but the sheer {{b2}} is still concerning. I've noticed my own ability to focus has got worse over the last few years. I struggle to read a book for more than twenty minutes before I feel the {{b3}} to check my phone.
Emma: I know what you mean — I've experienced that too. But I'm not sure screens are entirely to blame. Life has just become more {{b4}} generally. Open-plan offices, constant notifications, meetings that could have been emails. The problem might be broader than screens.
David: Maybe. But there's pretty {{b5}} evidence that social media in particular is linked to rising rates of anxiety and depression, especially in young people. Platforms are literally designed to keep you {{b6}}. That's not neutral technology — that's a deliberate design choice.
Emma: I don't disagree that some platforms are exploitative. But banning screens or reducing time arbitrarily doesn't solve the underlying issue, which is that people — especially kids — aren't being taught how to use them well. {{b7}} should be as fundamental as reading and writing.
David: Digital literacy, yes — but also just limiting usage. A lot of the research suggests that the happiest teenagers are those who spend significant time offline: in sport, in face-to-face conversation, in nature. Real-world engagement seems to be {{b8}}.
Emma: I completely agree with that. But the same research often shows that moderate screen use — say, one to two hours of social media — has no measurable negative effect on wellbeing. The dose makes the {{b9}}, as they say.
David: The dose makes the poison — sure. But who decides what a healthy dose looks like when the apps are optimised to make you consume as much as possible? It's naive to say 'just use it in moderation' when the whole product is designed to {{b10}} moderation.
Emma: That's fair. I think the responsibility does lie partly with the platforms. But I also think adults and parents can model better behaviour. If children see their parents constantly on their phones at the dinner table, what message does that send?
David: A terrible one. I'll admit I'm guilty of that myself. I think the honest answer is that we're all still figuring this out — society, parents, educators, tech companies. It's a very new problem.
Emma: Exactly. And I think the worst thing we can do is panic and overreact. Nuanced thinking — like what you're doing — is far more useful than blanket bans or moral panics.
David: Well, thank you. Though I suspect you might be complimenting me just to win the argument.
Emma: Maybe a little. But I do mean it.

blanks.explanation: 'Scrolling through social media' is the established collocation — 'scrolling' refers to the physical gesture of moving through a feed.
'Sheer volume' means the pure quantity of something — 'sheer' adds emphasis, suggesting the size alone is the problem.
'The urge to' suggests a strong, almost compulsive desire — more specific and intense than simply 'desire' or 'wish'.
'Fragmented' means broken into pieces — Emma is describing how life lacks continuity and is constantly interrupted.
'Compelling evidence' is a collocation meaning evidence that is convincing and hard to dismiss.
'Keep you hooked' is an idiomatic expression meaning to design something so people cannot stop using it.
'Digital literacy' is Emma's term — it refers to the ability to use technology critically and effectively.
'Protective' in a health context means that something acts as a shield against harm or disease.
'The dose makes the poison' is a well-known scientific proverb — 'poison' is part of the fixed expression.
'Undermine' means to erode or weaken something from within — David argues the design works against the user's self-control.

criticalThinkingQuestion: David and Emma both agree that platforms are designed to keep users engaged for as long as possible. If technology companies know their products are harmful to some users, do they have an ethical responsibility to design them differently — even if it reduces their profits? Or does the responsibility lie with individuals and parents?
